pending.body: `run stop` can report success while leaving backend subprocesses alive. In `src/cli/run.rs:2175-2213` and `src/cli/run.rs:2266-2303`, `kill_descendant_process_groups` only SIGKILLs descendant process groups whose PGID differs from the orchestrator’s PGID, and the forced-stop path at `src/cli/run.rs:2961-2978` only sends SIGKILL directly to the orchestrator PID itself. Ordinary backend children inherit the parent’s process group, so they are never signaled here; once the parent dies they keep running even though `run stop` proceeds to rewrite `run.json` as failed/resumable. The added regression only covers a `setsid` child (`tests/cli.rs:6255-6349`), so the default same-process-group case is still unhandled. The stop path needs to tear down same-PG descendants too, or refuse to claim a clean stop when it cannot guarantee backend teardown.

Non-authoritative legacy `run.pid` files are treated as stale even when the recorded process may still be alive. `src/cli/run.rs:1539-1585` falls back from a pid record without `proc_start_ticks`/start-marker to lease heuristics and returns `Stale` whenever there is no fresh lease. Both `run resume` (`src/cli/run.rs:1859-1867`) and the stale branch of `run stop` (`src/cli/run.rs:2871-2899`) then reclaim the lock and rewrite the snapshot as failed/resumable. The new test `tests/cli.rs:6353-6408` demonstrates this explicitly by treating a live `sleep 60` process as “already stale”. That allows a mixed-version/live legacy orchestrator to continue running while a second orchestrator is resumed against the same project.

Partial stale-owner cleanup is silently ignored before reacquiring the CLI writer lease. `src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:186-199` returns `Ok(false)` when reclaiming a stale daemon worktree owner only partially succeeds (for example the writer lock is released but worktree or lease-file cleanup fails). `src/cli/run.rs:1798-1807` discards that boolean and immediately retries `CliWriterLeaseGuard::acquire`. If the writer lock happened to be released before the partial failure, resume/stop will continue even though the stale daemon lease/task/worktree state was not fully repaired. Recovery should stop and surface that partial cleanup instead of continuing on top of contradictory durable ownership state.
